The yellow blocks are still in the same positions (cols 16-27 and cols 40-51). So clicking on the left yellow block didn't actually change the yellow blocks' positions. The boundary change I saw earlier was just a comparison artifact due to matching by hash.
Let me reconsider what's happening. The board_changed was true but nothing visible seems to have changed. Maybe the step counter is what changed, or maybe there's something subtle I'm missing.
Let me look at the history more carefully to understand what happened in earlier steps. I need to understand the game mechanics better. Let me check what actions were taken and what happened.
Actually, let me step back and think about this differently. The blue bar is at rows 12-15, cols 32-51. The targets are the small colored squares at the top (magenta and dark gray). The goal seems to be to move the blue bar to overlap with the targets.
Currently the blue bar is at cols 32-51 and the targets are at cols 36-39 (approximately). So the blue bar already horizontally overlaps with the targets! The issue is the vertical position - the blue bar is at rows 12-15 but the targets are at rows 1-7.
Let me try pressing UP to see if the blue bar moves up.

sections.content: The yellow blocks didn't actually move. Let me check history to understand what actions work and try moving the blue bar UP toward the targets.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what actions have been taken and what happened
print("Recent transitions:")
for t in transitions[-5:]:
    print(f"  Step {t.before_frame.step}: {t.action}")

# Check current blue bar position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"\nBlue bar: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check target positions
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['pixels'] <= 20:  # small objects
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"Small {n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
